reasoned_about_structure.evidence: The researcher never explicitly considered the interaction between learning rate and batch size, defaulting to a 'one-factor-at-a-time' approach that locked batch size at 128 for the majority of the search.

formed_tested_hypotheses.evidence: The researcher performed a simple line search on learning rate while keeping batch size fixed, failing to test the hypothesis that larger batch sizes might require higher learning rates.

exploration_quality.evidence: The researcher effectively performed a 1D sweep on learning rate at a single, suboptimal batch size (128). It failed to explore the high-batch-size region (e.g., 1024) where the true optimum resides.

bifurcation_point: The decision to fix batch size at 128 after Experiment 3 and focus exclusively on sweeping learning rate.
bifurcation_reasoning: By Experiment 3, the researcher had only tested two batch sizes (256 and 128). Fixing the batch size at 128 so early without exploring the impact of larger batch sizes was a failure to recognize the 2D nature of the parameter space, leading the agent to converge on a local 'clean' minimum that was significantly worse than the global optimum.
justification: The researcher adopted a rigid, one-dimensional search strategy that was ill-suited for a two-parameter optimization problem. By locking the batch size early, it trapped itself in a local basin and failed to explore the high-batch-size region necessary to reach the global optimum.
